data.propertyNotes: Permanent supportive housing operated on the Housing First model. No sobriety, income, or treatment prerequisites. Rent calculated at 30% of income, which is often $0 at move-in. Wraparound services on site. The limiting factor is the waitlist, not eligibility.

screeningBarriers: Background check — felony within 5 years disqualifies
data.propertyNotes: Matched through NEXTenant's landlord-partnership program. Has accepted vouchers and agreed to flexible screening for homeless-to-housed transitions. Deposit assistance runs through the NEXTenant side. Short walk to a Wichita Transit stop on Route 11.

screeningBarriers: No eviction on record within 3 years
data.propertyNotes: NEXTenant-matched. Landlord commits to working with case managers on tenant-level issues before escalating to eviction. Typically 1-2 of the 16 units turn over each month. Reputationally the landlord is considered flexible on formerly homeless tenants.

Downtown-adjacent property near the full stack of intake services. NEXTenant contributes deposit assistance and landlord mediation. Rent is above the voucher fair-market value, but walkability to Open Door and Center of Hope offsets the cost for case-managed clients.

screeningBarriers: Chronic-homelessness or high vulnerability score required
data.propertyNotes: HUD-VASH vouchers for homeless veterans. VA provides clinical case management alongside housing. Scattered-site model across Wichita. 52 veteran placements recorded in 2025. Operates more smoothly than most general-population pipelines, in part because the case-manager-to-client ratio is significantly lower.

Mid-market Wichita apartment that represents the baseline private-rental stock. Functionally inaccessible to almost anyone exiting homelessness because the combined credit, income, and background thresholds move in lockstep. Included in the dataset as the reference point for the mainstream market.

screeningBarriers: First month + last month + deposit due at move-in
Two years of documented rental history
data.propertyNotes: Northside property. Rent is below market but transit access is poor — the closest bus stop is 1.8 miles away. Does not accept vouchers. The two-year rental-history requirement is the blocker most clients exiting homelessness cannot clear.

screeningBarriers: Deposit equal to one month rent
data.propertyNotes: Small private landlord accepting HCV. Lowest rent in the dataset but sits in a south-Wichita submarket with limited transit, higher crime indicators, and distance from intake services. A clean illustration of the affordability-vs-access tradeoff — cheaper rent pushes clients away from the supports they still need.

screeningBarriers: No criminal history of any kind
data.propertyNotes: Downtown luxury stock. Transit and service access is the best in the city, yet the screening and rent combination makes the property inaccessible to anyone exiting homelessness. Included to show the flavor of growth currently displacing older affordable inventory downtown.

Affordable-housing development with project-based vouchers assigned to the property. No move-in deposit. Screening is lighter than the private market. Typical waitlist runs 4-6 weeks. Strong option for families pairing rapid rehousing assistance with project-based subsidy.
